characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: Adaptational Heroism: In the animated series, while still blissfully ignorant to the chaos he causes, there are times where Mr. Bean will often help people and even stops crime on some occasions (e.g. stopping the thieves from stealing teddies, including his, recovering a stolen treasure chest or simply stopping an art thief). He also apologizes more for his actions and tries to make things right when he realizes that he screwed up majorly. Adaptational Intelligence: He is more competent and less bizarre in the animated series (it helps that in this case,the whole worldis more cartoonish here, Bean seems less like aFish Out of Water). Not to say he has no moments of clarity and ingenuity in the live-action series, though (just significantly less). He also speaks more, especially in later seasons. Adult Sleeps with Stuffed Toy: Bean still sleeps with a teddy bear, as it is also hisCompanion Cube. Ambiguously Human: His mannerisms, bizarre unexplained abilities, and the fact that the intro of the TV series shows him falling from the sky, could imply that Mr. Bean may not be as human as he looks. The finale for the animated series' original run plays with this, by introducing anIdentical Strangerwho turns out to be a member of an alien race who lookexactlylike Bean and suggesting that Bean himself might be a member of it.Some episodes of the animated series, however,note"Camping" and "Young Bean", for example.seem to contradict this by showing that Bean has always been a regular human being who just never grew out of his childlike immaturity. Some episodes of the animated series, however,note"Camping" and "Young Bean", for example.seem to contradict this by showing that Bean has always been a regular human being who just never grew out of his childlike immaturity.

characters.name: Mr. Bean
characters.description: Adaptational Heroism: In the animated series, while still blissfully ignorant to the chaos he causes, there are times where Mr. Bean will often help people and even stops crime on some occasions (e.g. stopping the thieves from stealing teddies, including his, recovering a stolen treasure chest or simply stopping an art thief). He also apologizes more for his actions and tries to make things right when he realizes that he screwed up majorly. Adaptational Intelligence: He is more competent and less bizarre in the animated series (it helps that in this case,the whole worldis more cartoonish here, Bean seems less like aFish Out of Water). Not to say he has no moments of clarity and ingenuity in the live-action series, though (just significantly less). He also speaks more, especially in later seasons. Adult Sleeps with Stuffed Toy: Bean still sleeps with a teddy bear, as it is also hisCompanion Cube. Ambiguously Human: His mannerisms, bizarre unexplained abilities, and the fact that the intro of the TV series shows him falling from the sky, could imply that Mr. Bean may not be as human as he looks. The finale for the animated series' original run plays with this, by introducing anIdentical Strangerwho turns out to be a member of an alien race who lookexactlylike Bean and suggesting that Bean himself might be a member of it.Some episodes of the animated series, however,note"Camping" and "Young Bean", for example.seem to contradict this by showing that Bean has always been a regular human being who just never grew out of his childlike immaturity. Some episodes of the animated series, however,note"Camping" and "Young Bean", for example.seem to contradict this by showing that Bean has always been a regular human being who just never grew out of his childlike immaturity.

characters.name: Teddy
characters.description: Black Bead Eyes: He's drawn with these in the animated series. Butt-Monkey: Downplayed. Mr. Bean treats himmuchbetter than he does most humans, but this still doesn't stop moments where he's accidentally shrunk or gets his head gets ripped off. Companion Cube: Bean treats Teddy how a four-year-old would treat a teddy, and it's both hilarious and ridiculously cute. Stock Animal Diet: Since he resembles a bear, Mr. Bean often pretends that he prefers honey. In the pet show skit from "Hair by Mr. Bean of London", Bean rejects a giant bone that he receives as a prize and chooses a jar of honey for Teddy instead, and in "Missing Teddy" from the animated series, he chooses a jar of honey over one filled with strawberry jam while going shopping. Unexplained Recovery: Teddy's been shrunk in one episode, decapitated in another and covered with paint in another, and even hammered on a chair, but is always back to his original size and shape by the next.

characters.name: Mr. Bean's Car
characters.description: Companion Cube: Much like Teddy, the car is a treasured possession of Mr. Bean. Drives Like Crazy: Especially in the episodeThe Trouble with Mr. Beanwhere he dresses in the car while driving himself to the dentist, andDo It Yourself Mr. Beanwhere he drives seated in an armchair tied to the roof of the car after stuffing the inside full of things he bought. Free Wheel: Occurs every time he drives his car and meets up with the Reliant (see below) Removable Steering Wheel: It's his primary security measure. It works when a car thief tries to jack his car. Running Gag: Often times while driving, Mr. Bean encounters an unseen person driving a blue Reliant three-wheeler, which always winds up crashed, knocked out of its spot, or tipped over onto its roof.

characters.name: The Reliant Robin
characters.description: The Bad Guy Wins: The driver lets Bean go since all he wants is for the latter to havea taste of his own medicine. It eventually happens in the animated series. Beware the Quiet Ones: Sooner or later, Bean will eventually piss the driver off... Butt-Monkey: Mr Bean relishes the chance to cause it some distress. In any episode where the Reliant Robin appears, it will inevitably end up on its side by the end. A Day in the Limelight: Gets a major spotlight for "Car Wars" when the Reliant Regal driver has had enough of Bean's antics. The Dog Bites Back: Finally gets the upper hand on Mr. Bean in "Car Wars", first by pursuing him, then dropping the latter's beloved green car into a car crusher.

characters.name: Irma Gobb
characters.description: Adaptational Jerkass: In the animated series, Irma is noticeably less patient with Bean and more stubborn to go with being made less mature. She also tends to cheat on Bean more, which leads to hypocrisy in the episode "Wedding Day", where she crashes a wedding that Bean was taking the bride to because she thought he was being unfaithful, despite herself running off with another man in "Hot Date" and "Valentine's Bean". Adaptational Personality Change: Irma's maturity level is closer to Bean's in the cartoon, making it easier to justify their relationship. Amusing Injuries: After Mr. Bean frightens her, she passes out. When she comes to, she's got an X-shaped bandage on her forehead. Butt-Monkey: Not so much, but her 'boyfriend' rarely shows her genuine affection and she is prone to certain misfortunes. Flat Character: In the original live-action series, she didn't really have much of a character besides being Bean's girlfriend. The animated series gives her more of a personality, such as her shared immaturity with Bean and her job as a librarian, but not enough to avert this trope.

characters.name: Mrs. Wicket
characters.description: Berserk Button:Mr. Bean's shenanigans in general, especially when they end up going too far and end up impacting her. Also anyone hurting Scrapper is expected to be beaten. Butt-Monkey: While she is usually aKarmic Butt-Monkeywhen it comes to Bean's antics, especially if she was mistreating him beforehand, later seasons would have bad things happen to her even on her nicer days, or is barely doing anything wrong. The episode "Operation Wicket" has aRunning Gagof Bean injuring her swollen toe several times. Canon Immigrant: Her first appearance is a mere mention or two in the "novelisation" of the first filmMr. Bean's Scrapbook. The animated series makes her a regular character, though her son Ernest is nowhere to be seen. Instead, she has a nephew named Billy, who didn't appear until Series 3 and wasn't even given a name for 6 years until Series 4. Catchphrase:She often yells"BEEEEEAN!"whenever Bean's antics result in misfortune for her. She yells this more in later seasons.She also says "Right" and "Eh?" a lot, particularly in the early seasons. She often yells"BEEEEEAN!"whenever Bean's antics result in misfortune for her. She yells this more in later seasons.

characters.name: Scrapper
characters.description: Amazing Technicolor Wildlife: His fur is yellow with black and brown spots. Cats Are Mean: He despises Mr. Bean with a passion and often finds ways to inflict misfortune upon him. Even as a kitten, he's just as vicious and bad-tempered. And it's not just Bean, he's like that toeveryone. The only person he is friendly to is his equally surly owner. Cat Up a Tree: In "Cat-Sitting", a mishap with the microwave causes him to be launched through a window and land in a tree, forcing Mr. Bean to try to get him down. It fails spectacularly, asthe tree then shoots Scrapperinto outer space. Ear Notch: He has two of these on his right ear. Even the Dog Is Ashamed: When Mrs. Wicket is taken to the hospital in "Cat-Sitting", Scrapper looks genuinely upset at what happened to her and then glares at Mr. Bean for accidentally injuring her, likely thinking "This is all your fault!".

characters.name: Ms. Wince
characters.description: Butt-Monkey: She is often receiving the brunt of Wicket's wrath, and is treated rather poorly by her. Bean tends to inconvenience her as well, or even put her life at risk. Given Name Reveal: In "A Royal Makeover", according to a dialogue by Mrs. Wicket, it is revealed that her name isMary. Nice Girl: In stark contrast to Wicket, anyway; she's generally sweet and doesn't seem to mind Bean as much. Nightmare Fetishist: In "Scaredy Bean", Wince is shown enjoying a horror movie, laughing hysterically while watching it. Throw the Dog a Bone: She gets one when she defeated Mrs. Wicket in a game of croquette in "The Mole".

characters.name: The Bruisers
characters.description: Beware of Vicious Dog: The Bruisers have a diabolical black Doberman pinscher named Fang who often targets Bean (and his Teddy) and Scrapper. Big Eater: It's shown in "Litterbugs" that the entire Bruiser family have an absurdly huge appetite, eating and eventuallylitteringa huge pile of food in one sitting. Big Guy, Little Guy: Most of the time, it focuses on a big Bruiser Sr. and a little Bruiser Jr. Bratty Half-Pint: Bruiser Jr., as well as his siblings. In fact, Bruiser Sr. and his wife have to take turns in the night to calm their noisy baby down. The Bully: Bruiser Sr. is probably the animated counterpart of the bully from "Tee Off, Mr. Bean" in terms of nastiness, but played up significantly. The rest of his family aren't that much better.

characters.name: Billy Wicket
characters.description: Ascended Extra: Originally appeared in two episodes of Season 3, but appears more regularly in Season 4. Early Installment Character-Design Difference: In Season 3, heshared the same designas a generic little boy incidental. However, Season 4 would give him an older-looking, more unique design to go with hisAscended Extrarole. Given Name Reveal: His first name was unknown until Season 4, where it was revealed to be Billy. Intergenerational Friendship: WithManchildMr. Bean. Although Bean often upsets him, usually by hogging his toys, he acts as somewhat of a father figure to him, as they do activities together such as going to an Outward Bound course or building their own go-kart to compete with the Paliwals in a race. Morality Pet: His Aunt Julia loves him very much, just as much as she loves Scrapper.

characters.name: Mr. Paliwal
characters.description: Beware the Nice Ones: Paliwal is as nice as they come, but, in his debut episode, he takes the go-kart race too seriously, to the point of actively sabotaging Bean and Billy's chances of winning. He also outright loses it at the end of "The Sofa Stain" when he sees that Bean had stained his brand new sofa with chocolate. Funny Foreigner: He is of Indian descent, and often finds himself caught up in Bean's crazy shenanigans. Nice Guy: Compared to Bean's other neighbors, Paliwal is easy-going and friendly towards him, even if he can be a show-off. Remember the New Guy?: He wasn't given a proper introduction. He just shows up in "Karting Bean", and Bean talks to him like he had been living across the street for a while. The Scream: He lets one out in despair at the end of "The Sofa Stain" when he finds out that Bean stained his priceless white sofa.

characters.name: The Burglar Duo
characters.description: Annoying Laugh: The short one has a distinctively nasal laugh not unlikeSpongeBob SquarePants. Big Bad Duumvirate: You get not one but two antagonists in some episodes. Big Guy, Little Guy: Size does tell between the big and small burglar. Early Installment Character-Design Difference: In Series 1, each hadPerma-Stubbleand two ear piercings. From Series 2 onwards, their stubble was removed, as well as each losing one of their piercings. The large one also had brown hair in his debut, before it was changed to black for the rest of his appearances. Karma Houdini: Downplayed; many episodes have the duo get their comeuppance for their crimes, but there have been a couple of times where they don't get caught. In the episode "Wanted", the burglars snatched the key that Mr. Bean forged using a piece of broken tile to escape prison. And in "Homeless", they successfully scammed Bean for a house rental. In both episodes,they were never seen again.

characters.name: Mr. Pod
characters.description: The Ace: Unlike Mr. Bean, Pod is anexcellent cookand treats Irma better. Identical Stranger: He was this at first until it's revealed that they are of the same species. Identical Twin ID Tag: He and Mr. Bean look exactly alike, down to their undergarments, and the only way to distinguish them is from their Minis and their stuffed toys: Mr. Pod's Mini doesn't have a padlock for the driver side door and has a penguin doll whom he names Pingy.His race also owns stuffed toys of various animals to distinguish them between one another. I Want My Beloved to Be Happy: Near the end of the episode, despite his infatuation with Irma, Mr. Pod chose to drop Mr. Bean out from the spaceship after seeing her distraught over them leaving her, and gives her aLaser-Guided Amnesiaso that she can be happy with Bean. Last Episode, New Character: Downplayed, since he's technically a doppelganger of Mr. Bean, but he is introduced in the finale of the show's original 2002 run.

characters.name: Mime
characters.description: Big Bad: He acts as the main antagonist of "Mime Games", where he constantly annoys Beanjust for not leaving him a tip. The Gadfly: He trolls Mr. Bean in such mimetic ways, and evenfollows him homejust to annoy him some more. Suddenly Voiced: The mime is almost always silent, but when Mr. Bean outsmarted the mime who fell in the pond and the face paint washed off, he lets out an "Ohhhh no no no...." Your Mime Makes It Real: A few examples make some sense with this trope: Like Beam miming a lasso, tying up the mime and pulling him back to the park, or Bean mimicking aFinger Gunand "shooting" directly at the mime.

characters.name: The Restaurant Owner
characters.description: French Jerk: By telling his French accent and gesture, he is one who is obsessed withGreed; this sin is best shown in the episode "The Visitor", where he initially denies Bean and Harry entry to the restaurant as they're closing up for the night until Bean shows him a wad of cash. Lean and Mean: Downplayed. While not an antagonist, he still poses a skinny stature. Pet the Dog: Despite his harsh treatment of Mr. Bean throughout the episode "Restaurant", at the end of the episode when the Male Celebrity goes on a warpath against Bean after having served him carrots disguised as a lobster. Instead of being angry at Bean for making another mess, he gets concerned for him and even decides to lay off him after all that torture.

characters.name: David Langley
characters.description: Cluster F-Bomb: He gives one when he discovers Mr Bean vandalisedWhistler's Mother, spewing out a torrent of religious-themed curse words. Despair Event Horizon: He reaches this as a result of Bean's actions throughout the film, confiding in Bean that secretly he wishes he'd never been born.This prompts Bean to try and make amends for his actions. Fatal Flaw: His fundamental flaw throughout the film is his naivete and his refusal to conduct proper research. Had he conducted proper research, he would have discovered that Bean was sent to America on false grounds. Hair of Gold, Heart of Gold: He has curly, dirty-blonde hair, and is a very nice man. Horrible Judge of Character: He initially presumes that Bean is an academic expert, at least till he meets him. It's not his fault though, since the only thing he had to go on was a set of phony recommendations given to him by the British Royal Art Gallery's real academics.

characters.name: Alison Langley
characters.description: Awful Wedded Life: Even before Bean enters her life, she and David's marriage is already starting to get rocky. The only reason they return is out of sheer pity and even in her interactions with David, her tone is rather passive-aggressive. Defrosting Ice Queen: As with her adolescent daughter, she is rather cold towards Mr Bean at first, later derisively nicknaming him "[your] friend from the planet Zog" (a possible reference to the famous opening sequence of each episode of the television series, which has caused the common fan hypothesis that Bean is an alien, as is the case in the animated spinoff). However, as a result of himsaving her daughter's life, she begins to warm up to him and is touched by his gift of a box of eggs. Housewife: She appears to fulfill this function within the Langley domicile. Screw This, I'm Outta Here!: Since she's not used to Bean's eccentricities, she decides to leave and take their offspring to stay with her grandmother in an attempt to blackmail David into getting rid of him. Wet Blanket Wife: Allison isn't all that unreasonable in not wanting Bean to stay with them, but immediately taking the kids to her mother's place and later threatening to leave David for good over the matter does seem like a bit of overreaction. Even worse that she already acts like this before actually meeting Bean. (David implies during the bar scene that their marriage already wasn't going well, even before Bean's arrival.)

characters.name: Jennifer Langley
characters.description: All Girls Want Bad Boys: Given that she's dating a biker, she's probably in accordance with this trope, and it's probably as much out of misguided adolescent affection as it is out of a desire to piss off her father. Bratty Teenage Daughter: Jennifer fulfills this role within the Langley family. She's often acerbic, rebellious and rude. At the airport, while the others are eagerly awaiting Mr Bean's arrival, Jennifer can be seen sulking nearby. Dating What Daddy Hates: She's dating a Hell's Angel by the name of Stingo. We don't exactly know how their relationship progressedfollowing the motorcycle accident. Defrosting Ice Queen: At first she assumes Mr Bean will be deformed, since she holds very strong biases against British people, based solely on then-Prince Charles. Her impression is proven correct when she actually meets him. However, when Bean saves her life, she, along with the rest of the family, warms up to him, to the extent that, rather than being embarrassed, she's actually touched by Mr Bean's gift of a Barbie doll. Freudian Excuse: Given that her parents' marriage is on the rocks by the time the film starts, it's possible Jennifer's rebelliousness, including the fact that she's dating a biker, is a result of her acting out in response.

characters.name: Kevin Langley
characters.description: Actually Pretty Funny: He finds Bean's wiggling of his ears to actually be quite amusing. The All-American Boy: The '90s version of this trope, since he appears to be a well-behaved kid who loves his parents. His dress sense is very '90s, what with his cap on backwards and his denim shirt creating a sort of grunge look. Hidden Depths: Apparently, he has a secret collection ofCindy Crawfordpictures in his bedroom. The idea of someone as young as him enjoying what is, at best, revealing and at worst age-inappropriate is shocking, to say the least. Intergenerational Friendship: Unlike his sister and his mother, Kevin takes an instant liking to Bean and becomes one of the only two members of the familyat firstwho is actually nice to him. They purchase presents for each other in the form of attire — Bean gifting Kevin what is essentially a miniature version of his ownIconic Outfit, while Kevin purchases him a more adult-fitting version of his own attire, to Bean's considerable embarrassment. Nice Guy: He's one of only two members of the family - other than his father - to be nice to Mr Bean. Even when he's about to stay with his grandmother, he doesn't hold a grudge against Mr Bean, only repeating his mother's words verbatim.

characters.name: Lieutenant Brutus
characters.description: Badass in Distress: During the drive to the hospital, he gets shot trying to prevent a mugging and has to be taken to the hospital himself. Bait-and-Switch Accusation: He is present at the unveiling of the painting, and asks for a word with David. At first, it seems as though he's managed to catch on to the fact that theWhistler's Motheris a fake, however, he's actually here to inform David that his daughter'sbeen injured in a motorcycle accident. Da Chief: The head of the Los Angeles Police Department (or at least the highest-ranking member involved), he evidently has years of experience under his belt, since during the prison cell scene, he mentions that he's taken on gangs armed with AK-47s. Did Not Do the Bloody Research: Inverted example, since he uses the term "fruitcake" when describing Bean (in British slang, it means someone who's an eccentric or a weirdo, while in America it describes a homosexual). Justified by the fact that the film was written by English screenwriters, who were unable to research what "fruitcake" meant in American slang. Inspector Javert: Quickly cements himself as this to Bean's Valjean immediately after he's introduced — after all, Bean is placed in his custody, and, during their second meeting, clarifies that, should Bean step out of line again, he will have no alternative but to arrest him and deeply relishes the opportunity.

characters.name: Sabine
characters.description: Beauty Equals Goodness: A very attractive young French woman, but also a very nice individual. Cool Car: Certainly how Mr. Bean reacted upon seeing her driving a dead ringer to his own lime-green Mini Cooper. This is confirmed by the novelisationMr Bean's Guide to France. French Jerk: Averted. She's French, but she's also quite nice. Heroic BSoD: She experiences this when she realizes thatCarson Clay cut the scene which was to make her name. Hidden Depths: While she normally communicates with Mr. Bean in French, towards the end of the film it's shown that she can indeed speak English, since she interrogates him about his accidental kidnapping in his mother tongue without the slightest hint of a problem.

characters.name: Stepan Duchevsky
characters.description: Daddy's Boy: It's never made clear how famous his father is, but when Stepan accidentally gets kidnapped, he's apparently close enough to him to be devastated when they are accidentally separated. Duchevsky clearly cares deeply enough for his son to spark an entire countrywide manhunt, which makes the headlines. Gratuitous Russian: Justified as this is his mother tongue. Intergenerational Friendship: Becomes friends with the middle-aged Mr. Bean as they travel through France to reunite with his father. Kid Hero: He's around twelve years of age when he first meets Bean, and becomes the deuteragonist of the film. Missing Mom: It's never made clear what happened to his mother.

characters.name: Carson Clay
characters.description: Big Bad: Of the film, but only nominally. For one thing, he doesn't even have any animosity towards Bean whatsoever — yes, he does get annoyed when Bean meddles with the filming of his yoghurt advert, but that's only because Bean doesn't really understand what he's doing on that set. When he confronts Bean over his meddling with his pretentious art-film, he instead kisses him on the cheek and decides that he wants to keep making films after the same kind of fashion. It's All About Me: Being a Hollywood film director, he does have an absolutely massive ego. While everyone else falls asleep during his pretentious, surreal police drama, he's clearly shown enjoying it, holding up his hand in a chef's kiss gesture. That said, he does have some standards: during the scene where it's apparent that Sabine's role has been cut, he can be seen nodding his head to a woman on his left, implying that he cut the scene as a favour to his jealous wife. Large Ham: When you're played by the same actor asThe GreenGoblin, it's inevitable that you'll ham it up. Pet the Dog: In spite of hisPrima Donna Directorbehaviour and being angry when Bean ruins his movie, he quickly comes around and embraces Bean's additions to the film when it turns out the audience liked them. He even starts mimicking Bean's unusual method of zooming a camera by physically moving the device forward and back afterwards, as if he sees genuine competence as a film director in Bean as a result. Prima Donna Director: Displays ridiculous obnoxiousness when filminga yogurt commercialusing the backdrop of an action-packed WWII battle. Later, his filmoveruses monologueto the point that the audience of the premiere gradually falls asleep.
